trips.name: Lavender & the Luberon
trips.description: In June and July the Luberon plateau turns into an ocean of purple lavender stretching to the horizon, filling the air with an intoxicating fragrance that defines Provence more completely than anything else. Perched villages — Gordes, Bonnieux, Ménerbes — crown ridges above terraced vineyards and cherry orchards, while the Sénanque Abbey with its lavender field courtyard is one of France's most photographed images.
trips.cover_search_query: Gordes village lavender Provence France

trips.name: Côte d'Azur Classic
trips.description: The French Riviera is Europe's most glamorous coastline — a 115-kilometre arc from Menton to Cannes where billionaires' yachts crowd harbour after harbour and the mountain backdrop turns the Mediterranean into the most dramatically framed sea in Europe. Nice's old town, the corniches above Monaco, and the cap peninsulas sheltering the most exclusive villas on earth make this one of the world's great drives.
trips.cover_search_query: Nice Promenade des Anglais Côte d'Azur France

trips.name: Gorges du Verdon
trips.description: The Gorges du Verdon is Europe's answer to the Grand Canyon — a 700-metre deep slash through limestone plateaus where the Verdon river runs an astonishing turquoise-green colour visible from the cliff-edge road 700 metres above. Lac de Sainte-Croix at the gorge's western end is a vast reservoir of the same impossible blue that makes every photograph look like a computer rendering.

stops.name: Point Sublime, Gorges du Verdon, France
stops.label: Point Sublime

trips.cover_search_query: Gorges du Verdon turquoise river canyon France

trips.name: Alpilles, Arles & the Camargue
trips.description: This southern Provence loop connects three utterly distinct landscapes: the jagged white limestone Alpilles ridge with the hilltop citadel of Les Baux; Arles, whose Roman arena still hosts bullfights under the same stone arches where gladiators once fought; and the Camargue delta — Europe's great coastal wilderness of salt flats, white horses, and pink flamingo colonies.
trips.cover_search_query: Les Baux-de-Provence citadel Alpilles France

trips.name: Aix-en-Provence & Marseille
trips.description: Aix-en-Provence is the refined aristocratic counterpart to Marseille's raw, passionate energy — a city of baroque fountains, plane-tree lined cours, and the ghost of Cézanne at every corner of Mont Sainte-Victoire's flank. Marseille is France's oldest and most misunderstood city, a port of extraordinary character where bouillabaisse was invented, the Calanques provide coastal drama rivalling Corsica, and the waterfront MuCEM is one of France's finest museums.
trips.cover_search_query: Marseille Calanques limestone coves France

trips.name: Var & Saint-Tropez
trips.description: Saint-Tropez transformed from a quiet fishing village into the world's most famous party destination in the 1950s when Bardot arrived — and the village itself remains genuinely beautiful despite the crowds, with a natural harbour lined with coloured houses and a citadel giving views over the Massif des Maures. The Var hinterland behind the coast is a landscape of wine cooperatives, perched villages, and Cistercian abbeys that feel a world apart from the Riviera below.
trips.cover_search_query: Saint-Tropez harbour village Var France

trips.name: D-Day Beaches & the Battle of Normandy
trips.description: The beaches of the Calvados coast are simultaneously beautiful — wide arcs of pale sand backed by dunes and clifftop villages — and among the most historically charged landscapes in the world. Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword beaches stretch along 80 kilometres of coast where 150,000 Allied troops landed on 6 June 1944. The American cemetery at Colleville-sur-Mer, with its 9,387 white crosses overlooking Omaha Beach, is one of the most moving sites in Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Omaha Beach American Cemetery Normandy France

trips.name: Mont Saint-Michel & the Bay
trips.description: Mont Saint-Michel rises from the tidal flats of the Couesnon estuary with such improbable drama — a Gothic abbey crowning a granite island surrounded by Europe's fastest tides — that it seems more like a vision than a real place. At high tide the bay fills within hours to isolate the island completely; at low tide the light across the sand and mudflats turns every shade of grey and gold. It is France's second-most-visited site after the Eiffel Tower and justifies every superlative.
trips.cover_search_query: Mont Saint-Michel island abbey tidal bay France

trips.name: Honfleur, Étretat & the Alabaster Coast
trips.description: The Alabaster Coast runs north from the Seine estuary in a succession of white chalk cliffs that rise to dramatic arches and needles at Étretat — the same formations that Monet painted obsessively from the beach below. Honfleur's picturesque old harbour, where the tall slate-fronted houses were painted by Boudin, Monet, and Courbet, and its connection to the Impressionist movement makes it one of Normandy's most appealing small towns.
trips.cover_search_query: Étretat chalk cliffs arch Normandy France

trips.name: Pays d'Auge: Cheese & Cider Country
trips.description: The Pays d'Auge is the soft, prosperous heartland of Normandy — half-timbered manor houses, apple orchards, and the meadows where Camembert, Livarot, and Pont-l'Évêque cheeses were invented. The Route du Cidre winds between producers where you can taste calvados, cider, and pommeau in farm settings unchanged since the 19th century. This is the Normandy of cream sauces, Channel fish, and unhurried country pleasure.
trips.cover_search_query: Pays d'Auge half-timbered manor apple orchard Normandy France

trips.name: Rouen & the Seine Valley
trips.description: The Seine meanders in enormous loops through chalk hills from Paris to the coast, and the valley is lined with the great abbey ruins — Jumièges, Saint-Wandrille — that made medieval Normandy a centre of European civilization. Rouen is Normandy's capital, a city of Gothic splendour where Joan of Arc was burned in 1431 in a square that still exists; its cathedral façade is the most complex Gothic stone-carving in France.
trips.cover_search_query: Rouen Cathedral Gothic Normandy France

trips.name: Finistère: Land's End
trips.description: Finistère — 'the end of the earth' — is the wild western tip of Brittany where Breton culture is most deeply rooted, Celtic identity most fiercely maintained, and the Atlantic coastline most dramatically wild. Presqu'île de Crozon's cliffs are among France's finest coastal scenery; the parish closes of Pleyben and Guimiliau preserve some of the most intricate Renaissance sculpture in France in tiny country churchyards.
trips.cover_search_query: Pointe du Raz Atlantic headland Brittany France

trips.name: Saint-Malo & the Emerald Coast
trips.description: Saint-Malo is the corsair city — a granite-walled citadel projecting into the sea from which privateers plundered English shipping for centuries. Its rampart walk above wide sandy beaches is one of the great free pleasures of France. The Côte d'Émeraude east toward Dinard and Dinan has the brilliant green water that gives it its name, and medieval Dinan's preserved half-timbered town above the Rance river is the finest in all Brittany.
trips.cover_search_query: Saint-Malo walled city ramparts Brittany France

trips.name: Gulf of Morbihan & Carnac
trips.description: The Gulf of Morbihan is an almost-enclosed inland sea of more than 40 islands where the tidal currents are so complex that no two visits are the same. Carnac's 3,000 standing stones — the largest megalithic site on earth — march in alignments across the moorland above the gulf in rows that remain as inexplicable as the day they were raised 7,000 years ago. Vannes's medieval walled centre is the gateway to this extraordinary corner of pre-Christian Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Carnac standing stones megalithic Brittany France

trips.name: Pink Granite Coast
trips.description: The Côte de Granit Rose between Perros-Guirec and Trébeurden has the most extraordinary rock formations in France — enormous boulders of shocking pink granite, worn by millennia of Atlantic storms into shapes that resemble animals, faces, and abstract sculptures. The Sentier des Douaniers coastal path weaves between them at sea level; the Seven Islands nature reserve offshore hosts puffins and gannets in huge numbers.
trips.cover_search_query: Pink Granite Coast Ploumanac'h Brittany France

trips.name: Classic Loire Châteaux
trips.description: The Loire Valley châteaux are the finest expression of French Renaissance architecture — built by kings and nobles who wanted to combine the military castle with the Italian palace, resulting in Chambord's fantastical roofscape of turrets, the perfect symmetry of Chenonceau spanning the Cher river, and Azay-le-Rideau reflected in its moat. UNESCO has recognised the entire valley as a World Heritage Site, and cycling between châteaux remains one of France's great pleasures.
trips.cover_search_query: Château de Chambord Loire Valley France

trips.name: Anjou & the Western Loire
trips.description: The western Loire around Angers and Saumur has a different, quieter character from the famous châteaux country downstream — this is the land of tuffeau stone caves carved directly into the riverbanks and used as wine cellars, mushroom farms, and even troglodyte dwellings that are still inhabited today. Saumur's cavalry school, Angers's extraordinary Apocalypse Tapestry, and the great wines of Anjou complete a rich itinerary.
trips.cover_search_query: Saumur château Loire river France

trips.name: Touraine: Garden of France
trips.description: Touraine — the 'Garden of France' — is the Loire Valley at its most concentrated and lush, with Tours as its cosmopolitan centre. Villlandry's extraordinary Renaissance kitchen and ornamental gardens are the finest in France; Leonardo da Vinci spent his last three years at the Château du Clos Lucé in Amboise; and Loches's fortified royal city, perched above the Indre valley, is the most complete medieval ensemble in the Loire.
highlights: Leonardo da Vinci Clos Lucé Amboise
trips.cover_search_query: Château de Villandry gardens Loire France

trips.name: Alsace Wine Route
trips.description: The Route des Vins d'Alsace is one of France's most scenic wine roads — 170 kilometres of Riesling, Gewürztraminer, and Pinot Gris vineyards threading between half-timbered villages so well preserved they look like sets from a fairy tale. Riquewihr's walls have never been damaged by war; Ribeauvillé's three ruined castles loom on the ridge above; and the storks that nest on every chimney have become the symbol of this unique Franco-German border culture.
trips.cover_search_query: Riquewihr medieval half-timbered wine village Alsace France

trips.name: Vosges Mountains & the Crest Road
trips.description: The Route des Crêtes runs along the crest of the Vosges from Cernay to Wangenbourg, offering dramatic panoramas over Alsace to the east and Lorraine to the west from a ridge that formed the front line between France and Germany from 1914 to 1918. The rounded summits (Ballons) are covered with pine forests and high meadows called chaumes, and the valleys below are dotted with spa towns, castles, and crystal glassmakers.
trips.cover_search_query: Route des Crêtes Vosges mountain panorama Alsace France

trips.name: Colmar & the Alsatian Villages
trips.description: Colmar is the most beautiful city in France that most tourists have never heard of — a remarkably well-preserved medieval and Renaissance town where every street is a pastel confection of corbelled upper floors and window boxes. The Unterlinden Museum holds the Isenheim Altarpiece, one of the most powerful works of art in northern Europe. The surrounding villages of Eguisheim, Turckheim, and Hunawihr are equally preserved and equally beautiful.
trips.cover_search_query: Colmar colourful half-timbered old town Alsace France

trips.name: Metz, Nancy & the Fields of Verdun
trips.description: Lorraine's two great cities could not be more different: Nancy is one of the finest examples of 18th-century urban planning in Europe, its Place Stanislas a gilded Baroque jewel; Metz is an ancient cathedral city where the Gothic nave rises so high it qualifies as the third-tallest in Europe and where the Centre Pompidou-Metz brings contemporary art to the region. Between them, the Verdun battlefield preserves the landscape of WWI's most devastating engagement.
trips.cover_search_query: Nancy Place Stanislas gilded Baroque Lorraine France

trips.name: Côte d'Or Wine Road
trips.description: The Côte d'Or is the most hallowed strip of agricultural land in France — a narrow escarpment between Dijon and Beaune where the geology, aspect, and microclimate combine to produce the finest Pinot Noir and Chardonnay wines in the world. Every village name on the road — Gevrey-Chambertin, Chambolle-Musigny, Vosne-Romanée, Meursault — is a famous premier or grand cru, and Beaune's Hospices auction in November is the world's most historic charity wine sale.
highlights: Clos de Vougeot grand cru estate
trips.cover_search_query: Beaune Hospices wine Côte d'Or Burgundy France

trips.name: Vézelay & Northern Burgundy
trips.description: Northern Burgundy is the quieter, less wine-focused side of the region — its centrepiece is Vézelay, a hilltop Romanesque basilica of surpassing beauty from which the Second and Third Crusades were preached by St Bernard and King Louis VII. The Chablis vineyards nearby produce France's most mineral Chardonnay; Auxerre's Gothic cathedral presides over the Yonne river; and Tonnerre's L'Hôtel-Dieu medieval hospice preserves a thousand-year-old carved wooden ceiling.
trips.cover_search_query: Vézelay basilica hilltop Burgundy France

trips.name: Cluny & Southern Burgundy
trips.description: Southern Burgundy is the landscape of Mâconnais and Charolais — white Charolais cattle grazing below the extraordinary rock formation of Solutré and Vergisson, and the Mâcon area producing the white wines that bridge Burgundy and Beaujolais. Cluny Abbey was once the largest church in Christendom, presiding over a monastic network that shaped medieval Europe; today one tower remains standing above the village that grew from its shadow.
trips.cover_search_query: Cluny Abbey ruins Burgundy France

trips.name: Périgord Noir & Sarlat
trips.description: The Dordogne Valley in the Périgord Noir winds through the most concentrated sequence of castles, prehistoric caves, and medieval cliff-villages in France. Sarlat is the finest medieval town in southern France — a labyrinth of honey-coloured limestone alleys and Renaissance mansions that could serve as a film set, and has repeatedly done so. The Vézère Valley's prehistoric sites — Lascaux, Font-de-Gaume, Les Eyzies — are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
trips.cover_search_query: Dordogne River cliff villages Périgord France

trips.name: Lot Valley & Saint-Cirq-Lapopie
trips.description: The Lot river cuts through limestone gorges of extraordinary beauty from Cahors east to Saint-Cirq-Lapopie — voted France's favourite village and perched on a cliff so sheer above the river that André Breton lost his heart to it and moved here in 1950. The Célé valley and the Gouffre de Padirac — a vast underground cavern accessible by boat on an underground river — make this one of the most compelling drives in southwest France.
trips.cover_search_query: Saint-Cirq-Lapopie clifftop village Lot Valley France

trips.name: Bastide Towns of the Périgord
trips.description: The bastides are the planned medieval new towns of southwest France — built in geometric grid patterns with central covered markets in the 13th and 14th centuries as both French and English crowns tried to colonise the disputed land between them. Monpazier is the most perfect survivor; Domme crowns a ridge above the Dordogne with views that stop conversations; Villefranche-du-Périgord is famous for its autumn mushroom and chestnut market.
trips.cover_search_query: Monpazier bastide medieval village Périgord France

trips.name: Basque Atlantic Coast
trips.description: The French Basque Country has one of the most distinctive cultural identities in France — a language older than any other in Europe, a cuisine that inspired nouvelle cuisine, and a coastline that combines sophisticated resort towns with world-class surf beaches. Biarritz hosts the surf world championships in waves that draw riders from across the globe; Saint-Jean-de-Luz is a perfect Basque fishing town where Louis XIV was married in 1660.
trips.cover_search_query: Biarritz surf beach Basque Country France

trips.name: Hautes-Pyrénées & Gavarnie
trips.description: The Hautes-Pyrénées contain the highest and most dramatic mountain scenery in the French Pyrenees — the Cirque de Gavarnie is the greatest natural amphitheatre in Europe, a 3,000-metre wall of rock with a 423-metre waterfall and year-round snow. Lourdes draws millions of Catholic pilgrims to its Marian apparition sites; Cauterets and Luz-Saint-Sauveur are elegant 19th-century spa towns in deep mountain valleys.
trips.cover_search_query: Cirque de Gavarnie mountain amphitheatre Pyrenees France

trips.name: Cathar Country
trips.description: The ruined castles of the Cathar heretics perch on vertiginous limestone peaks throughout the Aude and Ariège — Peyrepertuse, Quéribus, Montségur — their walls seemingly growing from the rock itself, abandoned after the Albigensian Crusade of the 13th century. Carcassonne is the medieval fortress city that was never captured, its double ring of walls the most complete surviving example of medieval military architecture in Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Carcassonne medieval walled city Languedoc France

trips.name: Ariège & the Pyrenean Caves
trips.description: The Ariège valley in the French Pyrenees is one of Europe's richest areas for prehistoric painted caves — Niaux's Salon Noir contains some of the most perfect Magdalenian bison and ibex drawings in existence, painted 13,000 years ago. The valley rises through medieval towns and Cathar castle ruins to the Pyrenean crest at Ax-les-Thermes, a spa town known since Roman times for its sulphurous springs bubbling at 78°C.
trips.cover_search_query: Foix castle Ariège Pyrenees France

trips.name: Roman Languedoc
trips.description: The Languedoc was the heartland of Roman Gaul — the Via Domitia ran through here from Italy to Spain, and the Roman infrastructure remains extraordinary: the Pont du Gard aqueduct, the arena and Maison Carrée at Nîmes, and the theatre at Orange are all better preserved than most Roman monuments in Rome itself. The Canal du Midi, engineered in the 17th century, links this Roman landscape to the Mediterranean in another engineering marvel.
trips.cover_search_query: Pont du Gard Roman aqueduct Languedoc France

trips.name: Roussillon: French Catalonia
trips.description: Roussillon is France's most distinctly Catalan corner — attached to France only since 1659, its language, cuisine, and culture remain closer to Barcelona than to Paris. Perpignan's Palace of the Kings of Majorca and its vibrant café life feel resolutely southern; the Vermilion Coast's bizarre red porphyry rock formations at Agde and Collioure inspired the Fauvist painters who gathered there in 1905.
trips.cover_search_query: Collioure Fauvist harbour Roussillon France

trips.name: Hérault & Montpellier
trips.description: The Hérault département contains some of France's most diverse landscapes in a small area — the volcanic Cirque de Navacelles, the karstic Gorges de l'Hérault, the unique circular glacial lake of the Lac de Salagou, and the garrigue-covered hills of the Pic Saint-Loup wine appellation. Montpellier is France's fastest-growing city, with a superb historic centre and the Paul Valéry beach strip drawing half of France in summer.
trips.cover_search_query: Cirque de Navacelles canyon gorge Hérault France

trips.name: Chamonix & Mont Blanc
trips.description: Chamonix sits at the foot of Mont Blanc — at 4,808 metres the highest point in Western Europe — and the valley town has been the world capital of mountaineering since the first ascent in 1786. The Aiguille du Midi cable car is one of the most vertiginous rides on earth, ascending from 1,035 to 3,842 metres in 20 minutes for a view that encompasses three countries. The Mer de Glace glacier retreating visibly each year is one of the great demonstrations of climate change.
trips.cover_search_query: Chamonix Aiguille du Midi Mont Blanc Alps France

trips.name: Annecy & the Savoie Lakes
trips.description: Annecy has the cleanest lake in Europe and an old town of extraordinary charm — canals, flower-draped bridges, and medieval arcades built directly over the water make it one of France's most visited cities after Paris. The road circuits around Lac du Bourget (France's largest natural lake) and the thermal spa town of Aix-les-Bains complete a region of pre-Alpine beauty that attracted the Romantic poets and painters for a century.
highlights: Lac du Bourget France's largest lake
trips.cover_search_query: Annecy lake old town canal Alps France

trips.name: Écrins & Vercors
trips.description: The Parc National des Écrins protects the largest wild area in France — glaciers, ibex, and cols above 3,000 metres in a massif that most Alpine tourists overlook in favour of the more famous ranges. The Vercors plateau is completely different: a forested limestone massif with deep gorges and the wartime memory of the Maquis Resistance fighters who held out here until a brutal German reprisal in July 1944.
trips.cover_search_query: Vercors plateau cliffs Grenoble French Alps France

trips.name: Savoie & the Lake Geneva Shore
trips.description: The French shore of Lake Geneva runs from Geneva to Thonon-les-Bains in a succession of lakeside villages, vineyards, and castle ruins that back onto the Chablais Alps. The Chablais wine appellation produces crisp Chasselas whites from the steepest vineyards in France; Évian-les-Bains gives its name to the world's most famous mineral water and has a Palais Lumière belle-époque concert hall; the town of Yvoire is one of the most visited medieval villages in France.
trips.cover_search_query: Yvoire medieval village Lake Geneva Savoie France

trips.name: Bordeaux & Saint-Émilion
trips.description: Bordeaux's 18th-century riverside city is France's most elegant — a crescent of neoclassical stone facades sweeping along the Garonne in one of the greatest urban vistas in Europe. The great appellations of the right bank — Saint-Émilion, Pomerol — cluster around a perfectly preserved medieval hilltop town of limestone caves, carved church, and wine estates that produce the world's most expensive merlot-based wines.
trips.cover_search_query: Bordeaux Place de la Bourse riverside France

trips.name: Médoc Grand Crus
trips.description: The Médoc peninsula north of Bordeaux is the most prestigious wine-growing strip in the world — a flat, gravelly left bank of the Gironde estuary where Cabernet Sauvignon thrives in soils so poor nothing else would grow on them. The grand châteaux of Pauillac — Latour, Lafite-Rothschild, Mouton-Rothschild — are recognisable from their labels in every fine wine list on earth, and the Route des Châteaux allows visitors to drive between them and visit cellars.
trips.cover_search_query: Médoc wine château vineyard Bordeaux France

trips.name: Arcachon Bay & the Landes
trips.description: Arcachon Bay is a unique inland sea connected to the Atlantic — warmer and calmer than the ocean outside, its banks lined with oyster beds that produce the best flat oysters in France, eaten fresh on the quay with a glass of Graves white. The Dune du Pilat — the highest sand dune in Europe at 106 metres — looms above the bay's southern entrance, offering panoramic views over the Landes pine forest that stretches unbroken for 150 kilometres.
highlights: Dune du Pilat Europe's highest dune
trips.cover_search_query: Dune du Pilat Arcachon Bay Landes France

trips.name: La Rochelle & the Île de Ré
trips.description: La Rochelle's magnificent harbour, defended by three medieval towers and lined with arcaded streets, was France's foremost Protestant port and the launching point for the colonisation of Acadia in Canada. The Île de Ré — connected by a toll bridge — is a flat island of vine-covered dunes, salt marshes tended by paludiers, and white-painted fishing villages with blue shutters where Paris escapes for the summer, cycling between boulangeries on single-speed bicycles.
highlights: Île de Ré salt marshes and cycling
trips.cover_search_query: La Rochelle harbour towers Île de Ré France

trips.name: Champagne Country
trips.description: Champagne is the most famous wine appellation on earth — and the region that produces it is a landscape of rolling chalk hills covered with Chardonnay and Pinot Noir vines, dotted with Gothic cathedrals and the great houses (Moët, Veuve Clicquot, Taittinger, Krug) whose cellars tunnel for hundreds of kilometres beneath Reims and Épernay. Reims Cathedral is where 25 French kings were crowned; the cellar visits in Épernay are among France's great tourist experiences.
trips.cover_search_query: Épernay Avenue de Champagne vineyard France

trips.name: Troyes & the Champagne Lakes
trips.description: Troyes is the best-kept secret of northeast France — a medieval city so completely half-timbered it resembles a Flemish painting, its centre shaped like a champagne cork (intentional or otherwise). The Forêt d'Orient and the great reservoir lakes of the Aube plateau are France's largest artificial lakes, surrounded by forest and attracting vast numbers of waterbirds and water sports enthusiasts within an hour of Paris.
trips.cover_search_query: Troyes half-timbered medieval old town France

trips.name: Auvergne Volcanoes
trips.description: The Auvergne is France's volcanic heartland — the Massif Central plateau is dotted with extinct volcanoes, round-topped puys, and crater lakes called maars that form the most dramatic highland landscape in France. The Chaîne des Puys UNESCO Geopark contains 80 volcanic features within 35 kilometres; Puy de Dôme rises 1,465 metres above Clermont-Ferrand with views encompassing the entire volcano chain.
trips.cover_search_query: Puy de Dôme volcano Auvergne France

trips.name: Gorges du Tarn & Millau
trips.description: The Gorges du Tarn cut 500 metres through limestone causses between the Lozère and Aveyron plateaus — sheer dolomitic cliffs above a river of extraordinary translucent green, with medieval villages such as La Malène and Sainte-Enimie clinging to the canyon walls. The Millau Viaduct spans the Tarn valley on columns taller than the Eiffel Tower, visible from 20 kilometres in every direction as one of the great feats of modern engineering.
trips.cover_search_query: Millau Viaduct Gorges du Tarn France

trips.name: Cévennes: Stevenson Country
trips.description: The Cévennes is the wild Protestant mountain stronghold that sheltered the Huguenot Camisard rebels and later inspired Robert Louis Stevenson's Travels with a Donkey in the Cévennes in 1878. The Cévennes National Park is UNESCO-recognised for its outstanding natural beauty — chestnut forests, granite ridges, and deep-cut river gorges, with the Corniche des Cévennes offering one of France's greatest mountain panorama roads.
trips.cover_search_query: Cévennes mountain gorge national park France

trips.name: Ardèche Gorges
trips.description: The Gorges de l'Ardèche is the most dramatic canyon in France that can be traversed by canoe — a 30-kilometre paddle through rock arches, grottoes, and cliffs up to 300 metres high, ending at the Pont d'Arc natural arch. The Chauvet Cave nearby contains the oldest cave paintings in the world at 36,000 years — the official replica Caverne du Pont d'Arc allows full access. This is southern France at its most primal and spectacular.
trips.cover_search_query: Pont d'Arc natural arch Ardèche Gorges France

trips.name: Southern Corsica
trips.description: Southern Corsica concentrates the island's greatest landscapes in one arc — the extraordinary turquoise sea and granite boulders of the Bonifacio straits, the Alta Rocca highland interior of chestnut forests and prehistoric sites, and the Calanches de Piana's astonishing pink granite formations above the Gulf of Porto. Bonifacio itself perches on chalk cliffs above a natural harbour in a setting of almost absurd theatrical beauty.
trips.cover_search_query: Bonifacio chalk cliffs harbour Corsica France

trips.name: Northern Corsica & the Balagne
trips.description: The north of Corsica contains the island's most dramatic scenery and its wildest terrain — the Cap Corse peninsula is a miniature island-within-an-island of ancient Genoese towers and tiny fishing harbours; the Balagne is the 'garden of Corsica', a landscape of olive groves, villages perchés, and the medieval city of Calvi beneath its Genoese citadel. The Calanches de Piana's rose-coloured granite towers above the Gulf of Porto are among France's most spectacular natural sites.
highlights: Calanches de Piana pink granite UNESCO
trips.cover_search_query: Calanches de Piana pink granite Gulf of Porto Corsica France

trips.name: Côtes du Rhône Wine Road
trips.description: The Rhône valley wine road runs from Lyon south through the Northern Rhône's steep granite vineyards — Hermitage, Côte-Rôtie, Condrieu — to the broader Southern Rhône plateau of Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Gigondas. The hilltop village of Gigondas and the papal city of Avignon mark the southern end of one of the world's greatest wine journeys, with the ruined palace of the Avignon papacy as a dramatic finale.
trips.cover_search_query: Châteauneuf-du-Pape vineyards Rhône France

trips.name: Lyon & Beaujolais
trips.description: Lyon is France's second gastronomic capital — perhaps its first in terms of seriousness — where Paul Bocuse's influence still pervades a restaurant scene built on the bouchon tradition of hearty Lyonnais cooking in tiled bistros. North of the city, the Beaujolais hills are gentle, vine-covered, and scattered with wine villages that serve the new Beaujolais at Gamay's most joyful. Pérouges, one of France's best-preserved medieval hilltop villages, is a short detour.
trips.cover_search_query: Lyon Vieux-Lyon Fourvière hill France

trips.name: Paris & the Great Châteaux
trips.description: The Île-de-France surrounding Paris is the most densely historic region in France — within 60 kilometres of Notre-Dame lie Versailles (the world's largest royal palace), Fontainebleau (the original royal hunting lodge), Chantilly (the greatest private art collection in France outside the Louvre), and Chartres (France's supreme Gothic cathedral). Paris itself commands a lifetime's attention but even three days reveals the Louvre, the Eiffel Tower, and Montmartre at their finest.
trips.cover_search_query: Versailles Palace Hall of Mirrors France

trips.name: Opal Coast & Boulogne
trips.description: The Côte d'Opale between Calais and the Somme estuary is northern France's most dramatic coastline — great white chalk headlands, wide beaches backed by dunes, and the Cap Blanc-Nez and Cap Gris-Nez points from which England is visible on a clear day. Boulogne-sur-Mer has France's best fish market and a magnificent walled hilltop upper town; Le Touquet was the playground of Edwardian English aristocracy and retains its Art Deco charm.
highlights: Cap Blanc-Nez chalk headland England view
trips.cover_search_query: Cap Blanc-Nez chalk cliffs Opal Coast France

trips.name: Somme WWI Battlefields
trips.description: The Somme valley in Picardy was the site of the most costly battle in British military history — more than a million casualties between July and November 1916. The landscape is now peaceful rolling farmland, but the cemeteries, memorials, and craters are everywhere: Thiepval's immense arch bears the names of 72,000 missing soldiers; Beaumont-Hamel's Newfoundland Memorial Park preserves the original trenches in eerie near-perfect condition.
highlights: Historial de la Grande Guerre Péronne
trips.cover_search_query: Thiepval Memorial Somme WWI Picardy France

trips.name: Jura Wine & Comté Cheese
trips.description: The Jura wine region is the most original in France — producing yellow wine (vin jaune) in a style closer to dry sherry than anything else in Europe, as well as sparkling Crémant and the unique Vin de Paille dessert wine. The Comté cheese fruitières (cooperative dairies) dot the forested limestone plateau; Arbois is the hometown of Louis Pasteur; and the Reculée de Baume is one of the most spectacular natural amphitheatres in France.
trips.cover_search_query: Château-Chalon Jura wine village France

trips.name: Franche-Comté Lakes & Cascades
trips.description: The Franche-Comté plateau is Switzerland's French counterpart — a land of deep forests, limestone gorges, and glacially carved lakes that sit at the foot of the Jura mountains. The Cascades du Hérisson is the finest waterfall chain in France outside the Alps; the Grande Traversée du Jura cycling route passes through Besançon, whose enormous Vauban citadel is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
trips.cover_search_query: Cascades du Hérisson waterfall Jura France

trips.name: Haute-Provence & Verdon Hinterland
trips.description: The high plateau of Haute-Provence between the Verdon and the Alpine foothills is France's most complete lavender landscape — the Plateau de Valensole turns purple from mid-June to August in the most famous lavender field in Provence. Digne-les-Bains is a spa town ringed by geological oddities and the starting point of the Train des Pignes; the Préalpes de Digne are a UNESCO Geopark for their extraordinary geological diversity.
trips.cover_search_query: Valensole lavender plateau Haute-Provence France

trips.name: Marais Poitevin & Vendée Atlantic
trips.description: The Marais Poitevin is the 'Green Venice' of France — a vast marshland of flat-bottomed boats gliding through canals beneath a canopy of ash trees, once reclaimed from the sea by Dutch engineers in the 17th century. The Vendée coast offers some of France's finest sandy beaches and the island of Noirmoutier (accessible by causeway at low tide), whose salt marshes produce the famed fleur de sel and Bonnotte potato.
